joke.raw_joke: A woman tells her husband, "the clock fell off the wall and almost hit my mother in the head!" The husband replie - 'Shitty clock, always late!'

MILO_OFFICIAL_SCRIPT_v1

seed_id: seed-031
edit_level: rewrite
script:
  setup: "My wife said the clock fell off the wall and almost hit her mother."
  punchline: "Shitty clock. Always late."
  tag: ~
changes_made: Converted third-person framing to first-person for VO delivery. Cut "in the head" — redundant after "almost hit." Kept profanity — "shitty" is doing real work here, it sells the disappointment. Clean version ("that clock") loses the edge.
caption_notes: Setup as one card. Punchline as second card — "Shitty clock." and "Always late." can be one card or split into two beats depending on Iris concept. Single card preferred.
timing_notes: Setup delivered straight, casual, like telling a friend. 0.5s pause before punchline. Punchline deadpan — the humor is in the flat disappointment, not the anger. Emphasize "always."
tts_notes: ~
quality_flag: strong

RILEY_CASTING_PLAN_v1

):

1. Jerry Gold — Husband / VO narrator
   persona: Deadpan News Host
   why: The punchline is a single flat verdict. Jerry does not react. He evaluates. "Shitty clock. Always late." is not frustration — it is a ruling. Nobody delivers rulings like Jerry.
   notes: VO preferred. If on-camera: no expression, possible slow shrug. Not looking at his wife when he speaks.

2. Zoe Sparks — Wife (setup carrier)
   persona: Optimistic Teacher
   why: Zoe reads as genuinely alarmed, not performatively alarmed. The joke requires a real emotional setup so Jerry can flatten it. Her wide-eyed mid-report energy is exactly right.
   notes: Casual home wardrobe. No props. She is telling a story, not staging one.

3. Tasha Trend — Mother-in-law (near-miss victim, background)
   persona: Council Candidate (softened to Visiting Guest)
   why: Tasha carries inherent self-importance. She arrives with an agenda. The idea that a clock nearly hit her is funnier because she clearly feels the universe owes her better. Visually neutral relative to the joke target role — no uncomfortable racial subtext. Strong background anchor.
   notes: Remove any activist/campaign props. Upscale casual: blazer or structured cardigan, minimal accessories. Seated or standing near the wall, unaware of how close it was. Her baseline expression should read as vaguely dissatisfied with the visit so far — that is Tasha in a home environment without a podium.

Shot Intent:
- Setup: Clock-gap on wall visible. Zoe in foreground, mid-report, visibly distressed. Tasha in soft background — touching hair or adjusting collar where the clock almost hit, still slightly indignant about it.
- Punchline: Hold on Jerry. Full deadpan. Half-beat pause. "Always late." — if splitting to two caption beats, cut on that word. If single card, let the pause do the work.

Caption alignment (per Milo v1):
- Setup card: "My wife said the clock fell off the wall and almost hit her mother."
- Punchline card: "Shitty clock. Always late." (single card preferred)

Status: CAST LOCKED — ready for Iris visual concept.

concepts_count: 3

---

## Concept A — "The Living Room Report"
Style: Domestic sitcom, warm lighting, classic setup-punchline framing
Duration: 8.8s — Short and punchy. Two-beat rhythm (report → verdict).

Master video prompt:
"Warm suburban living room, afternoon light through curtains, cartoon illustration 2.5D style, clean bold lines, vertical 9:16 composition. Zoe Sparks (bubbly young woman, orange-and-magenta hair, teal glasses, teal jacket) mid-report, gesturing at a bare nail on the wall where a clock used to hang. Tasha Trend (auburn-haired woman, navy blazer, structured cardigan, vaguely annoyed expression) seated on a couch, touching the side of her head. Jerry Gold (brown-haired dad, handlebar mustache, teal jacket) in kitchen doorway, deadpan expression, coffee mug in hand. Caption-safe lower third."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.2s): HOOK — The Empty Wall
A warm suburban living room with afternoon sunlight coming through curtains. On the wall, a visible nail and dust outline where a clock used to hang. On the hardwood floor below, a round wall clock lies face-down, glass cracked in a starburst pattern. A small dent in the drywall near where it fell. Warm lighting, cozy but disrupted. No characters visible — just the evidence of what happened.

Scene 2 (2.2–5.0s): SETUP — Zoe's Report
Same living room. Zoe Sparks stands in the foreground — a young woman with short choppy orange-and-magenta two-tone hair, round teal glasses, teal jacket with a star pin, and hoop earrings. Her eyes are wide, mouth open mid-sentence, one hand gesturing toward the wall where the clock was. She looks genuinely alarmed. Behind her on the couch sits Tasha Trend — an auburn-haired woman with a side-parted wave, wearing a navy blazer over an olive collared shirt, small gold stud earrings. Tasha's hand touches the side of her head near her temple, expression reading as mildly indignant — she feels the universe owes her better. The cracked clock is still visible on the floor between them. Warm afternoon lighting.
Caption: "My wife said the clock fell off the wall and almost hit her mother."

Scene 3 (5.0–5.5s): THE PAUSE
A kitchen doorway in a suburban home. Jerry Gold stands in the doorway — a middle-aged man with tousled brown hair, a handlebar mustache, and a teal jacket over a golden-yellow hoodie. He holds a white coffee mug in his right hand. His face is completely neutral — no expression at all. Just processing. One slow blink. The kitchen is visible behind him, slightly out of focus. Neutral warm lighting.

Scene 4 (5.5–8.8s): PUNCHLINE — The Verdict
Same kitchen doorway, slightly closer framing on Jerry Gold (brown hair, handlebar mustache, teal jacket, coffee mug). His expression has not changed — same deadpan neutrality. Possible micro-shrug of one shoulder. He is not looking toward anyone. He stares slightly past camera. He takes a small sip of coffee. The delivery is a ruling, not a reaction. The comedy is in the complete absence of concern.
Caption: "Shitty clock. Always late."

Caption plan: 0.0–2.2s no text | 2.2–5.0s setup card | 5.5–8.0s punchline card (isolated)
Voice: Zoe urgent/breathless. Jerry flat/monotone. Emphasize "always." 0.5s pause between "Shitty clock." and "Always late."
Risk: Profanity LOW risk for YouTube Shorts. Fallback: simplify to two-shot (Zoe/Jerry) if wall-clock detail fails.
Jerry hook: "Clean, safe, classic two-hander. Default greenlight — nothing gets in the way of the punchline."
Vince: 9:16, 8.8s, 4 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline, Scene 2 setup+Tasha], trims=[Scene 1 can cut to 1.5s]

## Concept B — "The Newsdesk Verdict"
Style: Deadpan news broadcast, Jerry as anchor delivering breaking news
Duration: 9.4s — Slightly longer to sell the news format framing.

Master video prompt:
"News studio set, vertical 9:16, cartoon illustration 2.5D style, clean bold lines. Jerry Gold (brown-haired dad, handlebar mustache, teal jacket over dress shirt) seated behind a news desk, papers in hand, facing camera. Lower-third chyron graphic area. Studio lighting — flat, bright, slightly cold. Background: generic news studio with monitors. Deadpan local news anchor reporting on something absurd as though it's breaking news. Caption-safe zones in lower 20%."

Scene 1 (0.0–1.5s): HOOK — Breaking News Chyron
A television news studio set. Jerry Gold sits behind a curved news desk — a middle-aged man with tousled brown hair, handlebar mustache, wearing a teal jacket over a white dress shirt. He holds a stack of papers. The studio has flat, slightly cold lighting and blurred monitors in the background. At the bottom of the frame, a red "BREAKING NEWS" chyron banner reads: "CLOCK INCIDENT AT LOCAL HOME." Jerry looks directly at camera with an anchor's practiced composure. Professional, serious, completely disproportionate to the actual news.

Scene 2 (1.5–4.5s): SETUP — The Report
Same news studio, slightly closer framing on Jerry Gold at his desk (brown hair, mustache, teal jacket over white shirt, papers in hand). He reads from his papers with full anchor gravitas — serious face, slightly concerned brow. The monitors behind him glow soft blue. The red chyron at the bottom still reads "CLOCK INCIDENT AT LOCAL HOME." He is delivering this with the seriousness of a major breaking story. This is journalism.
Caption: "My wife said the clock fell off the wall and almost hit her mother."

Scene 3 (4.5–5.2s): THE PAUSE — Editorial Beat
Same news studio, very close framing on Jerry's face. He has set down his papers. His hand reaches toward his ear as if adjusting an earpiece. One beat of silence. His expression shifts almost imperceptibly — he has something to add. This next part is off-script. The red chyron has changed to: "DEVELOPING STORY." Studio lighting catches a slight gleam in his eye.

Scene 4 (5.2–8.2s): PUNCHLINE — The Editorial
Same close framing on Jerry's face in the news studio. The anchor mask slips — just slightly. The real Jerry surfaces for two seconds. His delivery drops the broadcast voice by 10%. This is personal now. A faint hint of actual disappointment. He does not blink. He does not look away. He is editorializing.
Caption: "Shitty clock. Always late."

Scene 5 (8.2–9.4s): TAG — Return to Anchor
Same news studio, pulled back to full desk framing. Jerry Gold picks up his papers, shuffles them professionally, looks at camera. The anchor mask is back on. He gives one slight professional nod. The chyron at the bottom reads: "MORE AT 11." The bit is over. He is an anchor again. Nothing happened.

Caption plan: 0.0–1.5s chyron only | 1.5–4.5s setup | 5.2–8.2s punchline | 8.2–9.4s no caption
Voice: Anchor voice for setup (measured, authoritative). Punchline drops anchor voice 10% — personal. "Back to you" tag returns to full anchor.
Risk: News desk may render inconsistently. Fallback: Jerry at a table with a microphone (podcast format).
Jerry hook: "Jerry gets the whole show. Deadpan News Host is his strongest persona. Format does the heavy lifting."
Vince: 9:16, 9.4s, 5 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 punchline, Scene 2 anchor delivery], trims=[Scene 5 can cut (saves 1.2s)]

## Concept C [SURREAL] — "The Clock's Confession"
Style: Courtroom drama meets absurdist animation — the clock is on trial
Duration: 10.2s — Needs extra time for transformation beat and visual payoff.

surreal_element: Anthropomorphic clock as defendant on trial. Jerry as judge. Clock literally arrives late — courtroom wall clock shows 3:00, defendant clock shows 3:15. "Always late" becomes a judicial ruling.
Weirdness budget: 6/10 (Pixar-meets-Judge-Judy, not full Dali)

Master video prompt:
"Cartoon courtroom, vertical 9:16, 2.5D illustration style, clean bold lines, warm colors. Jerry Gold (brown-haired dad, handlebar mustache) in judge's robes with gavel at elevated bench. Defendant chair: large anthropomorphic wall clock with cartoon arms, legs, and nervous expression, cracked face. Zoe Sparks at prosecutor podium pointing at clock. Tasha Trend in gallery touching her head. Courtroom wall clock behind Jerry shows 3:00. Defendant clock face shows 3:15. Dramatic courtroom lighting from above."

Scene 1 (0.0–2.5s): HOOK — The Courtroom
A cartoon courtroom, brightly lit from above with dramatic ceiling lights. At an elevated wooden judge's bench sits Jerry Gold — a middle-aged man with brown hair and handlebar mustache, now wearing black judicial robes over his teal jacket. A wooden gavel rests beside his hand. He looks down with zero sympathy. Below him in the defendant's chair sits a large anthropomorphic wall clock — a round clock with a cracked glass face, two thin cartoon arms gripping the chair arms nervously, two stubby cartoon legs dangling off the seat. The clock's hour and minute hands are spinning frantically, like nervous fidgeting. Its expression (formed by the clock numbers and hands) reads as guilty panic. Wooden courtroom interior, gallery benches visible in background.

Scene 2 (2.5–5.5s): SETUP — The Prosecution
Same cartoon courtroom. At the prosecutor's podium on the left stands Zoe Sparks — young woman with orange-and-magenta two-tone hair, teal glasses, teal jacket with star pin. She points dramatically at the defendant clock with one outstretched arm, mouth open mid-accusation, eyes blazing. Behind her in the gallery bench sits Tasha Trend — auburn hair, navy blazer, arms crossed, one hand touching the side of her head near her temple. She looks vindicated. The anthropomorphic clock in the defendant's chair has shrunk into itself, arms wrapped around its round body, cracked face tilted down in shame. Warm courtroom lighting.
Caption: "My wife said the clock fell off the wall and almost hit her mother."

Scene 3 (5.5–6.5s): TRANSFORMATION BEAT — The Verdict Looms
Close framing on Jerry Gold at the judge's bench in judicial robes. He raises a wooden gavel slowly above his head with his right hand. His expression is completely unchanged — this is a formality. Behind him on the courtroom wall hangs a large wall clock showing exactly 3:00. In the lower portion of the frame, the defendant anthropomorphic clock is visible — its cracked face clearly showing 3:15. The time mismatch is visible in the same frame. The defendant is literally late. The pun becomes visual before it becomes verbal.

Scene 4 (6.5–9.5s): PUNCHLINE — The Ruling
Same courtroom, dramatic low angle looking up at Jerry Gold at the judge's bench. He slams the gavel down — impact lines radiate from the strike point. His expression remains stone. The anthropomorphic clock defendant's cracked face splinters further, pieces of glass falling away from its round body as it flinches in the chair. Its cartoon arms fly up in shock. Behind Jerry, the courtroom wall clock still reads 3:00. The ruling is final.
Caption: "Shitty clock. Always late."

Scene 5 (9.5–10.2s): TAG — Tasha's Vindication
Cartoon courtroom gallery bench. Tasha Trend sits with auburn hair, navy blazer. She gives one single satisfied nod, chin tilting down and back up. A slight smirk crosses her face. Her arms are still crossed. She knew. Justice served. The anthropomorphic clock is barely visible in the background, slumped in the defendant's chair, face fully cracked.

Caption plan: 0.0–2.5s no text | 2.5–5.5s setup | 6.5–9.0s punchline | 9.0–10.2s no text
Voice: Zoe full courtroom prosecutor energy. Jerry judge voice — deeper, slower. Gavel syncs with "Shitty." Half-beat pause before "Always late."
Transformation beat: 5.5–6.5s — visual reveal that defendant clock shows different time than courtroom clock.
Risk: Anthropomorphic clock may render poorly (MEDIUM). Fallback: regular clock propped in defendant chair (inanimate defendant). Secondary: hard cut from living room to courtroom.
Jerry hook: "Weird on purpose. AI will make it weirder. That's the bit. Underneath it, Jerry delivers a verdict — his strongest move."
Vince: 9:16, 10.2s, 5 scenes, mustKeep=[Scene 4 gavel+punchline, Scene 3 transformation, Scene 1 clock reveal], trims=[Scene 5 can cut (saves 0.7s)]
